Premium to NAV Way Below Historical Average

When it comes to investing in business development companies, the ultimate question that investors have to ask is whether or not an existing net asset value premium is worth paying. In Hercules Capital's case, this net asset value premium currently stands at 31%, which is way (16%) below the historical net asset value premium of 1.56X.

Shares of BDCs have broadly declined recently, in part because of growing concerns about dividend coverage. Companies like FS KKR Capital (FSK) and Golub Capital (GBDC) have already lowered their dividends amid lower base rates and pressure on their net investment incomes. For Hercules Capital, an analyst downgrade from Piper Sandler weighed on sentiment last week as well, and Wells Fargo lowered its price target from $18 to $17.

I like the sell-off here quite a bit, especially because HTGC has such a strong coverage profile. Plus, the firm would not have declared special dividends if the regular dividend would be seen as risky. Over the next twelve months, I expect a rebound to the long-term valuation average of ~1.56X NAV, which implies a stock price target of ~$19 per share.

Risks With Hercules Capital

The biggest investment risk that I see for Hercules Capital is a decrease in excess dividend coverage. Currently, HTGC covers its payout well, but if loan defaults were to rise, the investment situation for the BDC could change. What would change my mind about HTGC is if the BDC were to see a drop in its dividend coverage ratio to 1.1X or lower, or if it were to scrap its supplemental dividends in the future.

Final Thoughts

Hercules Capital remains a top investment choice for investors that value dividend safety, a high yield, and strong NII performance. I believe that investors massively overreacted to the Q4 earnings report, especially because the declaration of new supplemental dividends highlights a high degree of confidence in Hercules Capital's income trajectory and portfolio performance.

Hercules Capital still has one of the best non-accrual percentages in the BDC industry, 0.1%, based off of fair value, and the company is growing rapidly. Lower interest rates in 2026 could further support this already strong origination growth. Based off of the 3 reasons I highlighted here, I believe that investors looking for a high-quality yield should consider buying the exaggerated sell-off.

data.title: Targa Resources: Industry Shrinkage Portends A Potential Recession And No Growth In The Future
data.description:
Targa Resources (TRGP) is a company that has location, location, and location. Additionally, it generally grows aggressively. During the current environment, that aggressive growth has been on the high side for a midstream company. But midstream generally follows upstream in terms of stock price performance. Plus, midstream usually grows mostly at one time during the best part of the cycle while catching up to upstream during the initial parts of a cyclical downturn. So, the average growth rate is going to include years that punctuate that rapid growth for a still decent but lower average growth rate. Usually, Mr. Market does not like that.

Stock Price

The stock price appears to be responding to the growth projects underway plus an acquisition.

Clearly, the stock price is fully priced with really no room for a cyclical downturn. That can be very dangerous because back in fiscal year 2020, there was a cyclical downturn, and the stock price lost roughly 75% of its value.

As the previous article noted, the company cut the dividend and ended up making debt repayment a priority. While the ambitious project schedule did manage to complete, balance sheet repair became the order of the day for some time to come.

Compare To Enterprise Products Partners

Compare this to a more modestly priced company like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD)

Enterprise Products Partners had no such worries back in 2020. Instead, this company just rolled on through 2020 with yet another distribution increase. With one of the highest debt ratings, there was no worry about repaying debt. Not only that, but the growth record is remarkably consistent as well, despite serving a notoriously cyclical and volatile upstream industry.

Enterprise Products Partners Common Units (which issue a K-1) did suffer a drop in 2020, but the stock has also quickly recovered its losses and remains on track to yield an average total return of that nice distribution plus some growth (for a total of) in the teens.

Where The Risk Lies

But when it comes to Targa, with a price-earnings ratio of 25, both companies completed growth projects during the cyclical downturn. That provided some growth until there were no more growth projects. Upstream does not need more capacity during a period of weak commodity prices. Instead, industrywide production tends to decline until commodity prices strengthen. It can therefore take a while for more expansion needs. That puts an end to rapid growth (and is the reason that Targa's growth is classified as cyclical growth), and it lowers the average growth considerably (usually with a zero growth year or two followed by some very slow growth).

But the Targa price-earnings ratio of 25 really does not allow for that cyclical downturn plus a few years of slow growth (let alone the dividend cut in fiscal year 2020). When that inevitable cyclical downturn begins in a low visibility industry like this, Targa stock is likely to reprice severely. Last time, the repricing cost the stock roughly 80% of its value.

Clearly the stock got hot again from fast growth. But the fast growth did not last the last time around. There is no reason to think that it will be different this time. It is time to be grateful to have the chance to ride a hot stock to a big gain.

Plus, what was an acceptable debt load during the good times became a priority to reduce during the cyclical downturn. The key here is that the market turned the focus from fast growth to the debt load all of a sudden. That led to dividend issues that the market hates with this kind of stock. The stock price sag back in fiscal year 2020 can be seen on the chart above. But the stock price was clearly on the weak side before that.

Currently, the stock price did pull back some until the latest news of an acquisition. The Stakeholder Midstream acquisition has now closed. As is typical for this company, the acquisition was made using debt (solely).

The third quarter reported a record of roughly $1.3 billion EBITDA. Undoubtedly the market is looking for still better results because of the acquisition and the growth projects underway.

Generally, a stock should be priced at about one times the growth rate when using the price-earnings ratio. In this case, that clearly is the case.

But with more than $17 billion in debt at the end of the third quarter, this company is pushing its limits. Admittedly, the latest $1.3 billion in EBITDA when annualized, gives a fairly conservative debt ratio.

Risks

The problem here in the past is that when a cyclical downturn happens, this company has an aggressive growth project schedule and sometimes an acquisition as well to complete (using debt). But the EBITDA growth slows due to the cyclical downturn. That causes what looks like a conservative EBITDA situation (due to rapid growth) to rapidly change in outlook until the recovery gets underway.

As a result, this is a stock that could deflate rather quickly to wipe out several years of growth. There is an old saying on Wall Street that "sometimes the bulls win and sometimes the bears win, but the pigs never win." We are definitely at that point here.

Also note that fast growth has its own risk for large companies in that the logistics can become daunting very quickly. A loss of quality control can be a major issue for a pipeline company at any growth rate if it happens.

Note that this company is now a $46 billion company in terms of market cap. Fast growth for large companies often does not persist. While the current price-earnings ratio is a little on the overvalued side, it is not far overvalued. But taking into account that large companies generally grow slower, if at all, the odds increase that the current growth rate will slow in the future.

Last Article

As the last article noted, the company cut its dividend back in fiscal year 2020 to repay debt. Clearly the rapid growth using liberal amounts of debt is setting the company up for a similar downturn situation.

The time to think about these things is when everything is going gangbusters. The stock is quickly becoming asymmetric to the downside in terms of future returns. This industry has not lost its cyclical nature.

That does not mean that the stock cannot go higher. It went nearly 20% higher than the last article. The old Wall Street saying about "the stock market can be irrational longer than you can stay solvent" applies here.

From what I can tell, the easy money has been made. The shareholder returns from the fiscal year 2020 low are fantastic (and probably more than anyone could reasonably expect).

Summary

To justify the current stock price, one would have to see years of growth ahead. But the Dallas Federal Reserve Energy survey, is showing an industry contraction for the second quarter in a row. In a free market, the cure for high prices is high prices.

"Activity in the oil and gas sector edged lower in fourth quarter 2025, according to oil and gas executives responding to the Dallas Fed Energy Survey. The business activity index, the survey’s broadest measure of the conditions energy firms face in the Eleventh District, remained negative, though relatively unchanged at -6.2 in the fourth quarter.

The company outlook index, while also still negative, improved slightly from -17.6 in the third quarter to -15.2, suggesting continuing pessimism among firms. Meanwhile, the outlook uncertainty index remained elevated and was relatively unchanged at 43.4."

This quote from the fourth quarter Dallas Federal Reserve Survey is the second quarter in a row to note the industry pessimism. If that does not change, then it is an extremely standard start to a cyclical downturn.

Back in the first quarter survey, the companies noted that they need WTI in the high $60s (except for big companies, which is a bit lower in the low $60s). At the time I covered in several company articles the industry frustration (which bubbled up in conference calls as well) about the administration's goal of a WTI in the $50s. The companies cannot drill profitably at the administration's goal, and that is before the effect of steel tariffs on the industry.

Furthermore, if you read the comments from senior industry executives about the survey, they are extremely frustrated with the current administration and its policies. One of the things that accompanies this is a whole lot of conservative budgets that are likely to lead to more quarters of activity contraction.

It is classic economics, that industry contraction will continue until oil prices strengthen sufficiently, or, as the industry executives tell investors, "the cure for low prices is low prices". That strongly implies another 2022 is in the making if the situation is not fixed. But first there will be an industry recession the way things are going.

That argues against continuing growth for a company like Targa in the long term. It could also mean debt issues again if the downturn is severe enough. If that happens, growth projects will be few and far between until the company recovers.

Understand that take-or-pay provisions protect against downside to some extent. But last time the midstream industry had a significant portion of customers that filed and liquidated. Also, that provision does not guarantee a growth rate during a cyclical downturn. So, while near-term earnings are somewhat predictable. Eventually a downturn takes effect.

With this company, the quarterly earnings are somewhat distorted whenever an acquisition is made, as there are one-time expenditures associated with that. There is also the issue of capitalizing costs to contend with. Therefore, the cash flow statement and EBITDA become very important to the analysis. For the time being they will likely continue to grow at least 20%.

The risk now is that the cyclical downturn that the market is worried about would wipe out several years of stock price appreciation. On the other hand, a price-earnings ratio expansion from current levels is possible but not likely.

While a 20% or so growth rate is attractive, it is very rare for something like that to continue throughout the business cycle. An overall growth rate of 10% to 15% throughout the business cycle is far more likely for a large company like this one.

That makes this idea a very strong sell. Obviously, it does not mean that the stock price cannot go up more. But it does mean that a very generous profit probably should be taken, as there is unlikely too much more to go in the current industry cycle. If anything, the price-earnings ratio indicates a cyclical peak (and a giant one at that).

Under risks, in order to justify that price-earnings ratio, there would have to be no more cyclical downturns. That is very much against the odds.

data.title: Marvell Technologies: Adding To Its Data Center Ambitions
data.description:
Shares of Marvell (MRVL) have been somewhat of a laggard in the semi industry over the past year. That is surprising, as the company has seen solid growth and delivered real operating leverage on the bottom line.

This rapidly helps to reduce valuation multiples, but these are still somewhat demanding. Perhaps more important in all this, is the decisive and forward-looking M&A action to further increase its exposure to, and capabilities in, the data center market. This makes me very keen to keep a close eye on this upcoming year.

A Fabless Semi Provider

Founded in 1995, Marvell's mission is to develop and deliver semiconductor solutions that move, store, and process data faster, more reliably, and more securely. Focusing on markets like data centers, carrier infrastructure, enterprise networking, the company makes the specific point that accelerating compute delivers on accelerated infrastructure.

Compute, in and of itself, is worthless, if the related interconnected and networking switching components are not fast enough to facilitate these speed advantages. In this, Marvell provides custom compute, switching, and interconnect products and solutions, not just used in data centers, but also in 5G and enterprise networking.

The company generated some $5.8 billion in sales in the fiscal year 2025, a number up in the mid-single digits from the year before, yet this came with GAAP operating losses of just over $700 million. This was explained by huge restructuring charges, amortization charges, and great R&D intensity, with the latter expenses alone pegged at nearly $2 billion.

That was a bit misleading as well, not only because of restructuring charges but also massive amortization charges, as otherwise substantial realistic profits were reported. At the time, data centers made up three-quarters of revenues, complemented by smaller exposure to enterprise networking, carrier infrastructure, consumer, and automotive industrial applications.

AI Kicks In

Driven by the explosive growth of the data center market, the company has seen spectacular growth over the past year, mostly corresponding with the fiscal year 2026. First quarter sales rose 63% to $1.90 billion, second quarter sales were up 58% to $2 billion, and third quarter sales rose by 37% to $2.07 billion. A part of the growth deceleration came after the company sold its automotive Ethernet business to Infineon Technologies AG in a $2.5 billion cash deal.

The company guided for fourth quarter revenues to move up to $2.2 billion, plus or minus five cents, despite the divestment of the automotive activities. Based on the third quarter results, we can also take a closer look at the profitability.

Non-GAAP earnings were reported at $0.76 per share, or $655 million in dollar terms. This, however, excludes $152 million in stock-based compensation expenses. This suggests realistic profits trending around $2 billion each year. Important to realize in this is that not all this momentum is driven by data centers. This data center share in relation to total revenues fell from 75% to 73%, despite the divestment of automotive activities, although data centers remain the overarching theme.

The 864 million shares of the company command a +$67 billion equity valuation here, for a roughly $69 billion enterprise valuation if we factor in $1.8 billion in net debt as of the third quarter. With revenues trending near $9 billion, the company trades near 8 times sales, and with realistic earnings trending near $2 billion each year, that translates into a demanding mid-thirty times earnings multiple.

Not Such A Great Boom

While many AI proxies and suppliers have seen massive returns over the past year, that is not really the case for Marvell, as shares actually peaked around the $120 mark when 2025 started, with optimism being high and premature versus other names. A current $78 share price is down a third, making the profile entirely different from other AI suppliers/beneficiaries.

This comes as Marvell has been a solid grower of the past decade, with reported revenues tripling, albeit accompanied by some 70% dilution over this period of time, severely limiting the advancements on a per-share basis. Much of this resulted from M&A moves, including a larger $6 billion deal for Cavium and an $8 billion deal for Inphi.

Flexing Its M&A Muscles

Adding to the strong momentum this year, Marvell announced a $3.25 billion deal to acquire Celestial AI, a pioneer in photonic fabric. This is a highly strategic deal designed to accelerate its capabilities in next-generation AI and cloud data centers. Note that this is a huge earn-out, potentially valued at $2.25 billion; this depends on the revenue milestones. This consideration is fully payable if and once cumulative revenues through fiscal 2029 top $2 billion, with a layered approach used up to that number.

This potential $5.5 billion deal was accompanied by another deal, as Marvell announced another $540 million deal to acquire Xconn in January, adding expertise in advanced PCIe and CXL switching silicon, adding to the switching portfolio of the business. Just like Celestial, this transaction is also earmarked to cater higher bandwidth and lower latency solutions to its networks, with anticipated revenue ramp-up to $100 million in the fiscal year 2028.

With much of the Celestial deal paid for with the issuance of stock, net debt is not a primary concern, although some dilution will be incurred. However, the combined price tag of both these deals comes in at $4-$6 billion, this being meaningful, but not an immediate game changer for Marvell. After all, these deals add to the future positioning and acceleration of the data center business.

All About The Valuation

I like the setup of a lagging stock in relation to growth in the business and certainly profitability, as well as forward-looking M&A action. The counterargument can be made just as well: why was this a >$100 stock a year ago given its performance at the time? So were investors too upbeat a year ago, and should we not read too much into the underperformance of Marvell's shares ever since?

In between the M&A activity, both on the buy and sell side, it is clear that the company actively works on its positioning here, adding to the growth profile. Comparing this to peers is hard. Industry leaders like Broadcom, Nvidia, TSMC, ARM, and a few others, command premium sales multiples in the teens, or higher.

Some laggards like Intel, NXP, Qualcomm, and some other firms trade at low single-digit sales multiples, with Marvell valued right in between. Comparing the gross margin, operating and sales growth profile, this seems about fair, with the business performing right between the group of laggards and industry leaders.

Based on all this, the valuation is relatively fair if we look at peers, but absolute valuations are demanding, with earnings multiples seen in the mid-thirties here, but again, premium multiples apply to the sector at large. Given the recent underperformance, the setup is rapidly improving here, making me more inclined to get involved, certainly if shares might see an unexpected dip, as volatility across the market has picked up a bit in recent weeks.

I like the decision to focus on the business and capital allocation moves a lot. Of course, the proof with recent M&A is in the pudding, and more undoubtedly will become clear as the year progresses, yet the direction of travel is positive. All this makes me keen to look at the upcoming performance and learn more about the recent M&A activity, as I do not rule out more acquisitions this year, being keen to revisit a neutral stance at such point.

data.title: MPLX Vs. Western Midstream: Choosing The 2026 Energy Renaissance Yield Leader
data.description:
Investment thesis

Demand for electricity, meaning natural gas, the key energy resource for power plants, will reach new record highs in 2026, bringing more attention to the midstream sector. Although this market segment offers a large number of assets with high and reliable returns, selection requires discipline in the allocation of financial capital. MPLX LP (MPLX) and Western Midstream Partners (WES) are examples of two conceptually mirror-image companies that, in the opinion of a large number of investors, share similar business strategies and asset structures. But this isn't really the case, making it possible for MPLX and WES to have different growth paths and market cap changes. The former is betting on an aggressive strategy of scaling its gas business profile by entering into new agreements with data center operators; the latter is focused on simplifying its structure, leverage, and implementing a broad business differentiation strategy. In addition to providing an in-depth comparison of the two companies' business models and strategies, this article aims to determine which of them is more attractive to investors focused on MLP assets.

MPLX Strategy Overview

MPLX is a diversified energy infrastructure company engaged in the collection, transportation, and processing of natural gas, oil, and petroleum products. The key facilities are located in the Permian Basin, Marcellus, and Utica fields, providing a steady stream of orders under long-term contracts that generate core EBITDA in the form of commissions from operators. Additionally, today MPLX is increasingly signing new contracts to supply energy resources to data centers. In fact, an agreement was recently signed to supply gas from Delaware to MARA Holdings (MARA) gas-fired power plants, which are expected to increase their capacity to 1.5 GW. An example of this deal reflects MPLX's tremendous prospects as supplies increase from the ongoing AI supercycle, contributing to growth in electricity demand.

The MPLX strategy is based on a combination of organic growth and an aggressive M&A policy. Harmon Creek III is the largest project, and its completion will increase gas processing by 300 MMcf/d. The other projects include the launch of the Secretaria processing plant and participation in the joint expansion of the Eiger Express pipeline with partners ONEOK (OKE), Enbridge (ENB), and WhiteWater. Through Matterhorn, which owns this pipeline, the MPLX will hold a 22% stake. Also, MPLX has made a bunch of acquisitions as part of its aggressive strategic development policy. Among them is the $2.375 billion deal to buy Northwind Midstream in August 2025, adding 150 MMcf/d of sulfur gas treatment capacity. During the second half of 2026, it is expected that the acquisition of the remaining 55% of shares in the BANGL NGL pipeline system will be finalized, increasing its capacity to 300,000 barrels per day.

Western Midstream Strategy Overview

Western Midstream is a diversified energy infrastructure company with a profile spread across natural gas, liquefied natural gas, and crude oil assets. Our client base is built around the operation of a core pipeline network located in the Permian Basin. It's the fastest-growing region in the US shale oil and natural gas market, helping to connect producers and markets. In addition, the WES has access to the transportation infrastructure of export hubs located on the Gulf Coast. Similar to MPLX, this company employs a combined approach to its business scaling strategy, which ranges from the implementation of its own projects to acquisitions in the M&A market.

The largest transaction for WES, for example, is the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions assets worth $1.5 billion, enabling the company to scale up its operations in the Delaware River basin. This acquisition aims to diversify the portfolio with a view to developing the water business. Western Midstream's asset portfolio includes approximately 830 miles of formation water pipelines. After the acquisition of Aris Water, the company added an additional 790 miles of pipelines. Production facilities for processing 1.8 million barrels of formation water per day were also added. These decisions are meant to lower the risks of depending on energy supplies. That said, some of Western Midstream's assets might be up for sale, because 43.45% of it is currently owned by Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

WES is building a new cryogenic plant, North Loving Train II, which will have a production capacity of 300 million cubic feet per day and is scheduled to start operating in Q2 2027. There are also plans to build the $400-450 million Pathfinder pipeline, that will carry more than 800,000 barrels of formation water per day to facilities in Loving County. This is set to start operating in early 2027.

Fundamental similarities between MPLX and WES

Both MPLX and WES are popular assets in the energy transportation sector. Upon analyzing their fundamental similarities, one can identify 4 key criteria that make them mutually exclusive assets in an investor's portfolio.

  • Revenue structure. Around 90% of the companies' revenues come from toll road models. These are made possible by contracts that reduce the risk of price volatility and have a fixed volume of energy resource transfers. These contracts are long-term, so the terms don't change that often. This means companies have a secure income stream that's not really affected by oil and gas market prices.
  • Corporate structure and taxes. Our chosen corporate structure, Master Limited Partnerships [MLP], frees the company from paying corporate tax, while profits are distributed among unit holders who get a K-1 form. This structure lowers the tax burden for investors but also makes tax reporting more complicated. Moreover, the MLP is not available to a number of institutional investors and is disadvantageous for non-US residents.
  • Strategic partnership. Each company has powerful strategic partners that provide a "moat." In the case of WES, this is Occidental Petroleum, whereas for MPLX, it is Marathon Petroleum (MPC). These partners guarantee stable utilization of the pipeline system and processing facilities, in which OXY and MPC are not only the main majority shareholders but also the largest customers.
  • Capital allocation policy. Both MPLX and WES follow a similar financial capital allocation strategy, influencing both payments and the ongoing financing of projects. The majority of CAPEX is typically covered by internal cash flow. Any excess funds may be used for share buybacks, which allows investors to recoup their capital.

Key differences between MPLX and WES

Even though MPLX and WES are seen as pretty similar companies in a lot of ways, taking a closer look at their business strategies and assets in 2026 shows they're actually pretty different. More in-depth analysis of the fundamental similarities between MPLX and WES reveals 4 main criteria that differentiate them. There are differences in business strategy, assets, distribution policy, and valuation.

Criterion #1. Business strategy

MPLX's strategy is based on asset diversification and vertical integration of divisions, but WES is sticking with its focus on being a pure Delaware Basin operator. To my mind, the MPLX has the upper hand because, aside from developing its pipeline system, they're rapidly growing their marine logistics for oil and oil products, expanding their list of deliveries and contracts. MPLX management is also focused on supplying energy to new data centers, confirmed by the contracts they've signed with MARA. By contrast, management at WES is developing a rather unrelated business line: water. It is a by-product of shale oil production, and its utilization and use can create a new source of income that will not depend on hydrocarbon production volumes or the volume of energy resources supplied through pipelines.

Criterion #2. Assets

Choosing the business strategies of MPLX and WES is closely tied to what assets they have. Let's check out the table below, where I've put together some info that shows how the companies differ.

Criteria

Criterion #3. Distribution policy

Distribution of profits among unit holders is a competitive advantage of MLPs for income-oriented investors. The yield on distributions is 0.99% higher at WES, and the process of increasing them is also more favorable. The average annual growth rate of distributions at WES over a five-year period was 23.95%, whereas at MPLX it was only 8.14%. Most of the increase in WES's payments was made in 2024 (by 52%), and now a moderate growth strategy is being followed. In the case of MPLX, an increase in payments was made not so long ago (in October 2025) and amounted to 12.5% at once. Given that this company maintains cash flow coverage at 1.3x-1.4x, the potential for growth in payments is higher for it. We may therefore see decisions in 2026 whereby MPLX's dividend yield will begin to increase compared to WES.

Criterion #4. Valuation.

Within the framework of company valuation, there is a slight difference. In terms of the EV/EBITDA multiple, the advantage in attractiveness lies with WES, since its shares have a level of 9.52x, whereas MPLX has 10.95x. In one sense, the MPLX shares are more overbought. But on the other hand, folks are willing to pay more for them for two reasons: how big the company is and its strategic growth potential with an aggressive business expansion strategy. The case of WES is that its specialization is narrower, while the development of its water business is overly differentiated for a midstream company. Thus, while WES's investment attractiveness is slightly higher according to a number of key metrics, my preference would still be for MPLX.

MPLX and WES Comparison Table

Risks of the investment thesis

In analyzing the risks of investing in assets such as MPLX and WES, note that although the impact of the price factor is minimized, the low price of gas and oil could lead to a decline in companies' revenues in the long term. Over time, those companies that extract energy resources may cease their previous activities, meaning that when contracts are renegotiated, less pipeline capacity will be reserved. In addition, it is my opinion that the price of MPLX and WES shares is strongly influenced by investor appetite for assets related to the AI sector. Failure to realize the potential for the AI supercycle to continue will lead to a reduction in electricity consumption forecasts. This could cause a decline in the consumption of energy resources such as natural gas, impacting both their prices and the volumes transported through pipelines. The potential for growth in MPLX and WES revenues and market capitalization therefore depends on the degree of risk associated with energy market pricing and the ability of the AI supercycle to continue.

Conclusion

Based on my analysis of the business strategy and business model of midstream companies such as MPLX and WES, my conclusion is that their assets are highly attractive for investment. For this reason, as part of my initial rating, the assessment will be "Buy." Nevertheless, in choosing between these two assets, my preference would be for MPLX, because it is a more reliable company and, regardless of its size, has the potential to demonstrate higher growth rates. In one sense, the MPLX and WES assets can be considered mutually exclusive for income-oriented investors. But on the other hand, they offer a choice between high-quality and financially disciplined energy infrastructure operators. If MPLX can be considered Midstream's "blue chip" whose potential has not yet been fully realized (given that many large-scale projects are expected to be launched, leading me to believe that this company's future income will be higher, which means higher distributions for unit holders), then WES represents a bet on maximum current yield, the growth of which is due to the early high efficiency of its operations in the Permian Basin. Furthermore, taking into account the rapid growth of the AI sector and the construction of data centers, it is MPLX that is the midstream company that can boast the first major contracts for the supply of energy resources to existing data center operators.

data.title: Celestica's Next Move Isn't About 2026 - It's About Proving 2027
data.description:
Celestica (CLS) has already more than doubled in the past year and has been consolidating for the past 4 months. This move was primarily driven by a rapid upward revision in revenue and EPS expectations following record bookings, stronger hyperscaler demand, and sustained guidance raises - rather than by speculative multiple expansion alone. The consolidation over the past four months therefore follows a period of unusually strong fundamental and price acceleration.

In this thesis I try to focus on whether this consolidation supports fresh entries on the stock with asymmetric reward odds, not the quality of the business. Generally, when a stock grows the way Celestica has (and many other AI stocks), the edge from identifying narrative potential at the lows is almost gone - in fact, expectations have generally risen in such cases. So, the core question now should focus on whether Celestica is building for the next leg of growth or distributing.

My analysis finds Celestica fairly priced. The obvious risks that are associated with a stock that has risen sharply in the recent past are not really as big a factor for Celestica. In fact, there are very good odds of EPS compounding better than expected in 2026. However, long-duration growth assumptions will need some more proof toward the middle of 2026 before shares again move up significantly. While risks are low, the consolidation has not increased the odds of another rally that doubles investment over the next year anyway. Hence, looking at the balance of risks and rewards, I find Celestica a Hold (tilting toward Buy). I expect a more moderate total return led by EPS beating expectations (already high) at best, rather than another round of fresh multiple expansion. So, waiting for growth numbers to support long-duration growth makes sense.

Collecting Evidence From Earnings Calls

I wanted to keep this thesis as objective as possible and not confuse business quality with stock quality. I started with evidence apparent from the past earnings call. The most recent earnings call (Q4 2025) shows the acceleration in AI networking demand is intact. HPS revenue was up 72%, 400G and 800G demand remained robust (800G ramping to account for more than half the mix), and pipeline buildup for 1.6T demand also looks supportive.

Even though the growth is still cyclical, it is now supported by a more secular and larger force of hyperscaler AI networking spend. And in that cycle, signs of no pipeline deterioration, and in fact accelerating bookings with visibility even into 2028, suggest we are more mid-cycle and not yet facing peak-cycle risks.

Since our Investor and Analyst Day this past October, the velocity and scale of awarded programs and growth opportunities for Celestica continues to expand.

We had a record bookings year in 2025 and we're really just building out to support those bookings. So there's very little risk in those programs materializing.

The visibility, Tim, that we're seeing with our customers at this stage is unprecedented. We're certainly into '27 and many customers were talking into 2028.

The revenue growth trajectory reinforces this view. Q4 revenues were up ~44% YoY and even ~14% sequentially, showing Celestica is still in a phase where revenue momentum is accelerating. The guidance of ~$4b at the midpoint for Q1 2026 shows the pace of growth holding up with an implied YoY growth of ~51%. Sequential growth may look a little slower at ~10%, but that is still decent given the scale difference we have now compared to a year ago (when quarterly revenue was at ~$2.7b levels)

I also see two dormant risks in hyperscaler concentration. The top 3 customers account for 63% of the revenue. This is expected, as there is a limit to deconcentration while growing in a world where leadership is narrow. Another risk to margins. Margins are currently supported by a strong HPS mix. If 400G slows faster than 800G ramps, the margins could get pressured - although there are no such evident concerns today.

Gauging Valuations and Finding an Edge

Fundamentally, I can see how growth is supported by a strong pipeline and long-duration bookings. The immediate trajectory into Q1 also looks great. However, at this stage of Celestica's journey, it is not enough to gauge whether growth will stay intact and strong. For a Buy to emerge, we have to have an edge that the valuation today is unaware of. And that edge is not merely strong growth but better-than-expected growth already assumed.

Technically that edge could come from better operating leverage and margins or growth, or both. Let us look at what is assumed at the moment. For 2026, analyst estimates show an expectation of ~40% YoY growth, i.e., full-year revenues of ~$17.38b, slightly above the company's own outlook of $17b. There are several ways to look at this expectation. Standalone ~40% growth in 2026 is higher than the great year Celestica had in 2025, but revenue still grew only 28% YoY - so an even better year in 2026 is already assumed. Another way to look at it is that the exit revenue run rate is already $4b (as per Q1 guidance, not Q4 2025 actuals). So annualizing that alone leads to $16b, with as little as ~5% sequential growth sufficient to meet both the company outlook and market expectations. That is, even if sequential growth normalizes significantly from current levels, we are on track. The revenue chart below shows that revenue is not only growing but still accelerating, and with evidence of strong demand signals indicated in Q4, there is no reason to challenge that trajectory.

There is, of course, an embedded drag in the revenue outlook that the market may be getting worried about, and hence the consolidation. And that is a slowdown later in 2026. While the Q1 growth is above 50%, the implied full-year growth is only 40%, implying slower quarters in the back end of 2026. That is a concern because long-duration growth assumptions for 2027 are another 40% growth on top of 2026.

One important reason why the growth appears to be slowing later on is tougher comps and a larger revenue base going ahead. While that in itself does not mean bad news for valuations, its long-duration impacts could be - such as underwriting slower growth in 2027. Management addressed these concerns and implied "pragmatic" assumptions built in there and did not indicate any real slowdown in demand. In fact, looking at Celestica's historical guidance levels and actuals delivered in 2025, high single-digit beats over guidance midpoints have been the norm. At the start of 2025, the revenue outlook was $10.7b. With sequential beats compounding, the overall revenues for the year came out as ~16% higher. So, simply extrapolating a ~16% beat again in 2026 reveals that much of an edge over market assumptions at the moment. However, I feel a more normalized expectation of 5-10% seems more appropriate given the larger base entering 2026.

The EPS guide into Q1 is also strong, with a 71% YoY EPS growth expected. However, the full-year outlook implies only ~45% YoY EPS growth. This is again driven by stiffer comps and a larger base rather than a loss in operating leverage. The adjusted operating margin outlook for 2026 is 7.8% (slightly higher than the 7.7% actually recorded in Q4). There could again be some positive surprises delivered here too if the HPS mix rises and ATS margins improve more than expected. But the overall impact of EPS improvements and revenue surprises, however high the odds, does not appear to exceed early double digits at best. That is the extent of the edge we have if valuations remain stable.

The forward PE ratio has compressed visibly from ~60x to ~30x. However, this compression has not come from share price corrections. This valuation compression has been because valuations are merely catching up with earnings, and the share price is going through a time correction at the moment. The forward year has also rolled over, increasing the earnings denominator. Importantly, at ~31x forward 2026 earnings and ~22x forward 2027 earnings, Celestica is no longer trading at speculative AI multiples. Neither do I have a high conviction of the valuation expanding again.

Celestica trades above AI platform names like Nvidia (20-30x EPS) and slightly below infrastructure names like Arista and Broadcom during strong cycles (30-40x EPS). These comparative ranges are for forward-year estimates around the end of 2026. However, Celestica’s operating margin is only ~7.8% versus ~20% and higher for those peers, limiting structural multiple expansion. At the same time, 40% revenue growth and ~40%+ ROIC makes a drop toward low-20s multiples unlikely, as growth disappointments are not visible today. That makes any significant valuation changes less likely in the near term. The baseline case therefore relies on an EPS edge, which is real but not exciting enough.

The real constraint on upside is underwriting 2027 growth. This is not improbable but needs evidence. It is possible if 800G remains strong while 1.6T ramps meaningfully (creating overlap instead of a clean and mutually exclusive transition). It is also possible if hyperscalers expand deployments within existing programs. However, my concern is denominator risk. At a ~$17b base in 2026, sustaining ~40% again requires large absolute dollar additions. Even a mild capex digestion phase or a timing shift in 1.6T could mechanically slow reported growth.

Even if 2026 delivers modest revenue and EPS surprises, the stock may remain range-bound until investors gain confidence that 2027 growth is durable. In my view, that means signals from Q1 or Q2 2026 that help support a similar growth in 2027. Until such data emerges, I expect Celestica to remain range-bound. The setup therefore favors patience rather than fresh longs at this stage. Hence a Hold.

data.title: Week Ahead: SCOTUS Decision On Tariffs? 8 Fed Officials Speak As The Market Discounts Almost 65 Bp Of Cuts This Year
data.description:
Last week began with the LDP's stunning victory in Japan. However, rather than sell-off as the market expected, the yen and JGBs rallied. It appears that the yen's use as a levered funding leg for carry trades had already been adjusted. At the start of the week, reports circulated that Chinese officials were encouraging local financial institutions to de-risk from US Treasuries. Yet despite it and the better-than-expected January US employment data, the 10-year Treasury yield fell nearly 15 bp last week, the most in five months. The yield slipped below 4.05% ahead of the weekend, the lowest in more than two months. The greenback fell against the G10 currencies last week, but consolidation seemed to be the main feature. At the end of the week, the futures market discounted nearly 65 bp of Fed cuts this year, up from almost 56 bp at the end of the previous week. This is to say, the derivatives market is discounting two Fed cuts fully and about a 60% chance of a third.

Although it is not in economic diaries, February 20 is a "decision day" for the US Supreme Court. It is possible that the court delivers its decision about the president's use of emergency powers to levy broad tariffs. Judging from the event markets, most expect the court to rule against the administration. As we have noted, some companies are reported to have sold their tariff refund claims to non-bank financial institutions for 40-50 cents on the dollar. Such a ruling may weigh on US yields, as a couple of rating agencies had cited the tariff revenue as credit positive. Although the House of Representatives approved a measure that would rescind the 25% emergency tariff on Canada over fentanyl, it seems largely symbolic. Even if it is approved by the Senate, the president will veto it.

US

Drivers: The market remains sensitive to any confirmation of the so-called debasement trade. News that Chinese officials are encouraging de-risking from US Treasuries hit the dollar hard. However, the unexpected strength in the January employment data helped steady the greenback. Still, after the CPI figures, the futures market is fully discounting two rate cuts this year and little more than a 50% chance of a third. The odds of a June cut, possibly the first that Warsh chairs, have eased to almost 85% from around 97% at the end of last week. Eight Fed officials speak this week, but the market seems to be more aggressive than most.

Data: It is a packed week for US data and is squeezed into four sessions due to Monday's holiday. The highlights include what will likely be soft January retail sales after the large drop in vehicle sales, an increase in manufacturing output despite the continued loss of jobs, and the first estimate of Q4 data. The Atlanta Fed's GDP tracker is 3.7% growth, while the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for 2.8%. Lastly, the December core PCE deflator is expected to have risen to 2.9% at the headline level from 2.8% in November.

Prices: At last week's lows, the Dollar Index retraced (61.8%) of its rally off the late January low. After the midweek low, DXY remained confined to Wednesday's range (~96.50-97.25). The 96.20-40 area may cap the upside. The momentum indicators are mixed, perhaps warning of continued consolidation in the coming days.

EMU

Drivers: US considerations seem to be the key force in moving the exchange rate. European developments seem scarce and steady. The ECB is widely seen on a protracted hold. There is a small bias in the swaps market toward a cut, but the odds are no more than around 15% through H1. The correlation changes in the exchange rate and interest rates (US two-year yield, German two-year yield, and the differential) are hovering near zero.

Data: With the eurozone's initial estimate of 0.3% quarter-over-quarter growth in Q4 25, matching the Q3 pace, the remaining December data, which this week includes industrial output, the current account, and construction output, is of passing interest. Germany's ZEW February survey is due February 17, but it rarely has much market impact. That leaves the highlight of the week, the flash February PMI on Friday. As of January, the manufacturing PMI was still below the 50 boom/bust level, while the service PMI was at 51.6. The composite has been above 50 since the end of 2024.

Prices: The euro rose to start the week but remained mostly within Monday's range (~$1.1810-$1.1925) for the rest of the week. The 20-day moving average is around $1.1840, and the euro has not closed below it in nearly a month. The euro's bounce stalled near the halfway point of the losses from the January 27 high (~$1.2080) and the February 6 low ($1.1765). The momentum indicators are mixed and not particularly helpful now.

China

Drivers: Chinese officials continue to sanction a gradual appreciation of the RMB. Beginning last May, the daily reference rate rose in only one month (September). Last week was the 12th consecutive week the fix has been lowered. In fact, a week in late November is the only week that the fix has risen since the end of September. This seems to be more a signal that some estimate that the state-owned banks are intervening on behalf of the PBOC.

Data: Chinese markets are closed from February 16 through February 23 for the Lunar New Year.

Prices: With no onshore yuan trading next week and near its best levels in several years, the market may be reluctant to push the offshore yuan far from where it was trading when the onshore market closed (~CNH6.9065). The dollar's range last week was roughly CNH6.8910-CNH6.9330.

Japan

Drivers: The market was positioned for an LDP victory in the February 8 election. The outcome was anticipated to be good for Japanese equities and negative for JGBs and the yen. While the equity call was correct, the JGBs and yen were bought, not sold. It turned out that lower yields did not prevent yen strength. Market positioning seems to be a more important driver of the exchange rate than interest rates presently. The rolling 30-day correlation between changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the Dollar Index bottomed last month near 0.50 and reached 0.80 last week, the highest since last October. It is now a little below 0.75.

Data: The busy week for Japanese economic data kicks off with the first look at Q4 25 GDP early Monday. The world's third-largest economy is expected to have grown by around 0.5% in the quarter after it contracted by 0.6% in Q3. Consumption seems to have remained soft, but business spending likely expanded, and net exports may have contributed in a small way after it shaved 0.2% off Q3 GDP. The January trade balance is due, and it has deteriorated without fail at the start of the year. It recorded a surplus of JPY113.5 bln in December. At the end of the week, Japan's January CPI will be reported shortly before the preliminary February PMI. We already know from the Tokyo figures released in late January that moderating food and energy prices will likely drag the headline rate below 2.0% for the first time since March 2022. The market typically has a muted response to the PMI series, but for the record, Japan's January composite PMI was 53.1.

Prices: In what appears to be a classic "sell the rumor, buy the fact" type of activity, the yen defied expectations after Takaichi led the LDP to a historic victory and rallied. In fact, last week's approximately 2.8% advance was the yen's biggest weekly rally since November 2024. The dollar found support near JPY152.25, a little above last month's low (~JPY152.10). The momentum indicators warn of the risk of further dollar losses, but we are less convinced. A break could signal a move toward the JPY150.80 area, which hosts the (61.8%) retracement of the dollar's rally from last September 17, when the Fed delivered its first of three rate hikes in 2025, and the 200-day moving average, which the dollar has not settled below since October 3. A move above JPY154.50 may be an early indication that a low is in place.

UK

Drivers: The immediate risk that UK Prime Minister Starmer is forced out has receded, and that appears to have taken some pressure off sterling, as an idiosyncratic factor. The dollar seems to be driving the exchange rate. The 30-day rolling correlation between changes in sterling and the Dollar Index is around -0.80. This year's range has been about -0.70 to -0.85. The 30-day correlation of changes in sterling and the UK's two-year yield is near 0.20, and correlation with the US two-year yield is only slightly lower.

Data: The three most market-significant economic reports are due this week: jobs, CPI, and retail sales. Government finances (January budget) and the preliminary February PMI also will be released. The dovish hold by the Bank of England on February 5 saw the market boost the odds of a hike at the next meeting (March) to a little more than 70% from about 20% before the meeting. Any weakness in prices and/or activity will likely lift the chances.

Prices: After jumping from about $1.35 to $1.37 in two sessions (February 6 and February 9), sterling traded mostly sideways. It had the dubious honor of being the worst G10 currency last week, rising by about 0.20% against the dollar and falling by about 0.25% against the euro. It briefly traded at a four-day low ahead of the weekend, a little below $1.3600. A close below $1.3600 weakens the technical tone, while it may take a move above $1.3730 to indicate an upside break.

Canada

Drivers: The rolling 30-day correlation of change in the Dollar Index and in the US dollar against the US dollar has approached 0.85, the highest since June 2024. The inverse correlation of the exchange rate changes and Canada's two-year yield is about -0.42. Last March, it recorded 2025 extreme near -0.65. It reached almost +0.40 in early October 2025. The 30-day correlation of changes in the exchange rate and the US two-year note is near zero. It has not been above 0.20 since last October.

Data: Canada saw the foreign appetite for its stocks and bonds wane last year as the merchandise trade deficit grew three-fold. The December updates are due this week. Through November, the merchandise trade deficit stood at C$30.6 bln compared with C$8.9 bln in the first 11 months of 2024. Foreign portfolio net inflows were about C$121 bln in the January-November period, down from C$179 bln in the comparable 2024 period. Canada also reports December retail sales, coming after a 1.3% surge in November (1.7%) excluding auto sales. The January CPI is on tap as well. Headline inflation finished last year at 2.4%, with the core at 2.5%. Bank of Canada Governor Macklem has suggested that the underlying core measures (median and trimmed mean at 2.5% and 2.7%, respectively) may be exaggerated. The Bank of Canada is widely understood to be on hold, and the bar to another cut seem high.

Prices: The US dollar recorded a bullish outside up day in the middle of last week as it recovered from a test on CAD1.3500. Follow-through buying lifted it to almost CAD1.3640 ahead of the weekend. This largely met the (61.8%) retracement objective of the decline from the February 6 high (~CAD1.3725) to the midweek low. The 20-day moving average is near CAD1.3660, and the greenback has not settled above it since January 21. A move above it could signal a return to the CAD1.3750 area.

Australia

Drivers: The bull case for the Australian dollar is predicated on the prospects of tighter monetary policy, as a G10 proxy for China, and its exposure to commodities, especially the metals. The rolling 30-day correlation with changes in gold is near 0.80, the highest in two years. It is around 0.48 with copper. It peaked last June at twice as much. The inverse correlation with the Dollar Index is around -0.70, the most extreme since last October. The correlation of changes in the Aussie and Australia's two-year yield is a little below 0.25. It has not been above 0.40 for nearly three years. The correlation with changes in the two-year US yield is a little above 0.40. Except for a brief period last month, the counterintuitive positive correlation has been the case since the middle of last October. Last year, it was inverted in January and February and was positively correlated between March and late May, when it became inverted until early Q4.

Data: The minutes from the recent meeting at which the central bank lifted the target rate by 25 bp will be reported early Tuesday, followed by Q4 wage data the following day. On Thursday, the January employment data will be reported. In 2025, Australia's full-time positions increased by an average of 8.3k a month. In 2024, the average was about 22.3k a month. The unemployment rate stood at 4.1% in December 2025 and 4.0% in December 2024. The participation rate eased to 66.7% at the end of 2025 from 67.1% at the end of 2024. Lastly, at the end of the week, the flash February PMI will be released. Recall that in January, the composite PMI jumped to 55.7 from 51.0, the highest since April 2022, with new orders and employment rising. Separately, the Reserve Bank of New Zealand meets early on February 18. Its easing cycle is over, and the swaps market has a hike fully discounted by the end of October.

Prices: The Australian dollar peaked on February 12, slightly shy of $0.7150, a three-year high. Profit-taking kicked in, and the Aussie fell to about $0.7045 before the weekend. The momentum indicators are mixed. Chart support is seen in the $0.7000-20 area. It would seem that much of the good news has been discounted in the Aussie's nearly 6% rally in the first half of Q1. The four-week rally in tow is the longest since June-July 2024.

Mexico

Drivers: The dollar remains stuck in its trough against the Mexican peso. It is generally true for many Latam currencies, including the Brazilian real, and the Colombian and Chilean pesos. Two shared characteristics stand out: relatively high rates and commodity exposure. The Mexican peso may also benefit from mostly global liquidity (admittedly it is not evenly distributed) and relatively low volatility. Over the past 30 sessions, changes in the US dollar against the peso are more inversely correlated with the JP Morgan Emerging Market Currency Index than the MSCI Emerging Market Currency Index (~-0.80 vs. -0.38). To dollar-based investors, between the yield pickup and the peso's gains, the return so far this year is about 5.7%. It is 4.7% for euro-based investors and 3.3% for yen-based investors.

Data: There are two notable reports in the coming days, and both are due in the second half of the coming week. On Thursday, the record from the recent Banxico meeting that seemed to confirm an extended pause in policy will be reported. On Friday, December retail sales will be announced. In November, they rose by an unexpectedly sharp 1.0%. That helped lift the average gain in the first 11 months of 2025 to 0.4%, compared with a 0.1% decline in the January-November 2024 period.

Prices: The dollar remained stuck in its trough against the Mexican peso last week. It traded in a range of about MXN17.1265 to MXN17.2820. The high was recorded ahead of the weekend. Given the strength of the yen and the resilience of the peso, it appears that the long peso/short yen carry trades were already adjusted. President Trump reportedly indicated he is considering ending the USMCA, which he negotiated in his first term and had said it was the best trade deal ever. The market hardly reacted as it looked more like a negotiating ploy. Continued consolidative trading, even if choppy, seems to be the most likely near-term scenario.
